county_metadata.record_losses: No known record losses. Young county with intact records.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1907 from Chatham and Moore counties. Pre-1907 records split between both parent counties. One of NC's youngest counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with Lee County ROD online portal. For pre-1907, check Chatham County (1771) and Moore County (1784). FamilySearch has records from 1907.

source_profiles.source_name: Lee County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search from county formation.
Wills and estate records from county formation.
Marriage registers from county formation.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fires 1878 and 1880 destroyed most court records. Land records from 1737 survived. Also destroyed early Johnston County records that had been transferred via Dobbs County.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Dobbs County (extinct) 1791. Dobbs was formed from Johnston 1758. When Johnston's records were transferred to Dobbs and then to Lenoir, the 1878/1880 fires destroyed those early records too. Pre-1791 records in Dobbs County papers; pre-1758 in Johnston and Craven counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch deeds — land records from 1737 survived the fires. NC State Archives has burned county resources. Census records from 1800 are complete. Adjacent county records may fill gaps.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Land records survived the 1878/1880 fires. Among the earliest surviving deed records in eastern NC.
Surviving wills. Some gaps from courthouse fires.
Marriage registers from 1851. Pre-1878 bonds partially lost.

source_profiles.source_name: NC State Archives — Lenoir County Burned County Resources
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State Archives holdings critical for this burned county. May contain materials deposited before fires.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1779 when extinct Tryon County was divided — Lincoln (eastern part) and Rutherford (western part). Pre-1779 records in Tryon County papers. Tryon was formed from Mecklenburg 1768. Parent of Gaston (1846), Catawba (1842), Cleveland (1841).
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch deeds and wills. Lincoln County ROD has good online portal. Gaston-Lincoln Genealogical Society covers both counties. For pre-1779, check Tryon County records at NC State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search from county formation.
Wills, inventories, and estate accounts from county formation.
Marriage bonds from 1779 and registers from 1851.
Active society covering both Gaston and Lincoln counties. Publishes 'Footprints in Time' quarterly.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Haywood 1828. Parent of Jackson (1851), Clay (1861), Swain (1871). Pre-1828 records in Haywood County. Mountain county with Cherokee history — Trail of Tears (1838) era records relevant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch and Macon County ROD online. Pre-1828 research in Haywood County. Macon County Historical Society has local collections. Census records from 1830.

source_profiles.source_name: Macon County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search from county formation.
Wills and estate records from county formation.
Marriage bonds and registers.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse burned during Civil War 1865. Most early records destroyed. Surviving records primarily post-1865. Shelton Laurel Massacre (1863) occurred in this county.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Buncombe and Yancey 1851. Pre-1851 records in both parent counties. The 1865 fire makes pre-war research very difficult. Mountain Appalachian county with deep-rooted families.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch for surviving records. NC State Archives critical for burned county research. Pre-1851 research requires Buncombe and Yancey county records. Census records from 1860 onward. Madison County ROD has online portal.

source_profiles.source_name: Madison County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search. Post-1865 records most complete.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1865 deeds largely destroyed in courthouse fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Surviving deed records — significant gaps pre-1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Courthouse fire 1865 destroyed most early records.

source_profiles.source_name: NC State Archives — Madison County Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State Archives holdings critical for this burned county. Alternative records may bypass courthouse storage.
Marriage records. Pre-1865 bonds largely lost in fire.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fires in 1862 and 1884 destroyed many records. Marriage records have gap pre-1872.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1774 from Halifax and Tyrrell counties. Pre-1774 records split between both parent counties. The dual fires (1862 Civil War, 1884) make early research challenging.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch for surviving records. Martin County ROD has online portal. For pre-1774, check Halifax and Tyrrell county records. NC State Archives may have pre-fire materials. Census records from 1790 complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Martin County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search. Some gaps from courthouse fires.
Digitized microfilm. Surviving deed records.
Marriage registers from 1872 forward. Pre-1872 marriage bonds destroyed in fires.

source_profiles.source_name: NC State Archives — Martin County Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State Archives holdings for this burned county. Essential for pre-fire records.
